stem: A connected planar graph drawn in the plane has [[v]] vertices and [[e]] edges. By Euler's formula \( V - E + F = 2 \), how many faces (including the unbounded outer face) does the drawing have?
solution: Rearranging Euler's formula gives \( F = 2 - V + E = 2 - [[v]] + [[e]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This solves for \(V\) instead of \(F\), swapping the signs of \(V\) and \(E\).
This drops the \(+2\) from Euler's formula entirely.
This drops the \(+2\) and also flips the sign of the difference.
This uses \(+2\) with the wrong sign convention, adding \(E\) and \(V\) together.
This overshoots the correct face count by 1.
This undershoots the correct face count by 1.

stem: A connected planar graph drawn in the plane has [[v]] vertices and [[f]] faces (including the unbounded outer face). By Euler's formula \( V - E + F = 2 \), how many edges does the graph have?
solution: Rearranging Euler's formula gives \( E = V + F - 2 = [[v]] + [[f]] - 2 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of \(F\) instead of subtracting 2.
This forgets to subtract 2 from the sum of vertices and faces.
This drops the face count entirely instead of adding it.
This flips the sign of the whole expression instead of solving correctly for \(E\).
This overshoots the correct edge count by 1.
This undershoots the correct edge count by 1.

stem: A connected planar graph drawn in the plane has [[e]] edges and [[f]] faces (including the unbounded outer face). By Euler's formula \( V - E + F = 2 \), how many vertices does the graph have?
solution: Rearranging Euler's formula gives \( V = 2 + E - F = 2 + [[e]] - [[f]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This flips the sign of \(E\) and \(F\) instead of solving correctly for \(V\).
This drops the \(+2\) from Euler's formula entirely.
This drops the \(+2\) and also flips the sign of \(F\).
This uses \(-2\) instead of \(+2\) when rearranging Euler's formula.
This overshoots the correct vertex count by 1.
This undershoots the correct vertex count by 1.

stem: A simple planar graph has [[n]] vertices (with \( [[n]] \ge 3 \)). What is the MAXIMUM possible number of edges it can have while remaining planar?
solution: Every simple planar graph on \( [[n]]\ge 3 \) vertices satisfies \( E \le 3V - 6 \), so the maximum edge count is \( 3([[n]]) - 6 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the tighter bound that applies only to BIPARTITE planar graphs, not general planar graphs.
This forgets to subtract the constant 6 from the bound.
This uses the complete-graph edge count instead of the planar edge bound.
This multiplies the constant 6 into the vertex count instead of subtracting it afterward.
This overshoots the correct maximum by 1, which would violate planarity.
This undershoots the true maximum by 1.

stem: A simple planar BIPARTITE graph has [[n]] vertices (with \( [[n]] \ge 3 \)); recall that a bipartite graph has no odd cycles, in particular no triangular faces. What is the MAXIMUM possible number of edges it can have while remaining planar?
solution: Since a bipartite graph has no triangles, every face uses at least 4 edges, tightening Euler's bound to \( E \le 2V - 4 \), so the maximum is \( 2([[n]]) - 4 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the looser bound for general (not necessarily bipartite) planar graphs.
This forgets to subtract the constant 4 from the bound.
This uses the complete-graph edge count instead of the bipartite planar bound.
This multiplies the constant 4 into the vertex count instead of subtracting it afterward.
This overshoots the correct maximum by 1, which would violate planarity.
This undershoots the true maximum by 1.

stem: What is the chromatic number of the cycle graph \( C_{[[n]]} \) (a single cycle on [[n]] vertices)?
solution: An even cycle is bipartite and needs only 2 colors, but an odd cycle forces a third color to avoid a same-color clash where the cycle closes; so \( \chi(C_{[[n]]}) = 2 + ([[n]] \bmod 2) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes the cycle is always bipartite, ignoring what happens when [[n]] is odd.
This assumes the cycle always needs a third color, ignoring what happens when [[n]] is even.
This uses the vertex count itself instead of the actual chromatic number.
This reports only the parity bit (0 or 1) instead of adding it to the base of 2 colors.
This overshoots the correct chromatic number by 1.
This undershoots the correct chromatic number by 1, which would not properly color an odd cycle.

stem: What is the chromatic number of the complete graph \( K_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: Every pair of vertices in \( K_{[[n]]} \) is adjacent, so all [[n]] vertices need pairwise distinct colors: \( \chi(K_{[[n]]}) = [[n]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This undershoots by 1, which would force two adjacent vertices to share a color.
This overshoots by 1, using more colors than necessary.
This assumes every graph can be 2-colored, ignoring that \(K_{[[n]]}\) is far from bipartite.
This confuses the chromatic number with the total number of edges in \(K_{[[n]]}\).
This overshoots the correct chromatic number by 2.
This undershoots the correct chromatic number by 2, leaving many adjacent pairs sharing a color.

stem: The wheel graph \( W_{[[n]]} \) consists of a cycle on [[n]] rim vertices plus one hub vertex connected to every rim vertex. What is the chromatic number of \( W_{[[n]]} \)?
solution: The hub needs a color different from every rim vertex, and the rim cycle itself needs \( 2+([[n]]\bmod 2) \) colors, so overall \( \chi(W_{[[n]]}) = 3 + ([[n]] \bmod 2) = [[answer]] \) (3 when the rim is even, 4 when the rim is odd).
distractor_rationales: This forgets to add the extra color needed for the hub, using only the rim cycle's own chromatic number.
This guesses a small fixed value with no relation to the actual coloring argument.
This wildly overshoots the true chromatic number for a wheel graph.
This wildly overshoots the true chromatic number for a wheel graph.
This wildly overshoots the true chromatic number for a wheel graph.
This wildly overshoots the true chromatic number for a wheel graph.
